heroOverview: Rhinosporidiosis is a chronic granulomatous infection of mucosal surfaces — predominantly the nasal cavity and nasopharynx — caused by Rhinosporidium seeberi, a waterborne organism now classified as an aquatic protistan pathogen rather than a true fungus. It is endemic in South Asia (especially India and Sri Lanka) and parts of sub-Saharan Africa, where prolonged wading in stagnant ponds and rivers is the primary exposure route. The disease produces soft, vascular, friable polyps that bleed easily, obstruct breathing, and expand progressively if untreated. While rhinosporidiosis is almost always confined to the mucosa and submucosa, it can involve the eye, skin, and — in rare cases — visceral organs. The only effective treatment is surgical excision, and antifungal drugs do not work because the pathogen is not a true fungus.

keyStats.prevalence: Exact global prevalence is unknown; India accounts for the majority of reported cases with thousands documented; Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, and parts of Africa and South America also report significant case clusters
keyStats.demographics: Peak incidence in males aged 10-40; male-to-female ratio approximately 4:1 in most Indian series, reflecting occupational and recreational water exposure
keyStats.avgAge: Most commonly diagnosed in children, adolescents, and young adults who swim or wade in stagnant fresh water
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated tens of thousands of cumulative cases in the literature; Tamil Nadu, India, reports the highest density of cases; rare cases imported to Europe and North America

definition: Rhinosporidiosis (ICD-10: B48.1) is a chronic, localised, proliferative infection of mucous membranes caused by Rhinosporidium seeberi, a unique organism now classified as a member of the Mesomycetozoa (DRIP clade) — a group of protists that sit at the animal-fungal boundary, distinct from true fungi, bacteria, or protozoa. R. seeberi cannot be cultured in any laboratory medium; its large sporangia (up to 350 µm in diameter), each containing thousands of endospores, are the diagnostic hallmark visible on histopathology of excised polyps. The organism's primary reservoir is believed to be stagnant freshwater and muddy sediment; humans are infected via transnasal inoculation during bathing or diving. The nasal passage is affected in over 70% of cases, with the nasopharynx, soft palate, and conjunctiva as next most common sites. The infection produces a characteristic pedunculated or sessile polyp: vascular, strawberry-textured, pale pink to red, which bleeds easily on contact. Growth is persistent and progressive without treatment, and autoinoculation can extend the infection to adjacent sites. Disseminated rhinosporidiosis involving skin, bones, viscera, and genitourinary tract has been reported in approximately 5% of cases, almost exclusively in immunocompromised individuals. It is classified under infectious disease and managed jointly by otolaryngology and ophthalmology depending on the site.

typesClassification.description: The most common form, accounting for over 70% of cases; produces pedunculated or sessile polyps arising from the nasal septum or inferior turbinate, presenting with unilateral nasal obstruction and epistaxis.
Infection of the conjunctiva, lacrimal sac, or eyelid producing a vascular granulomatous mass; the second most common site; causes tearing, foreign body sensation, and conjunctival injection.
Polyps arising in the nasopharynx or soft palate, potentially causing post-nasal drip, voice change, dysphagia, or, in children, snoring and sleep disturbance.
Rare; involves skin of the face, trunk, or genitalia via autoinoculation or haematogenous dissemination; presents as verrucous papules or nodules rather than true polyps.
Visceral or multi-site involvement (bones, genitourinary tract, liver); very rare, predominantly in immunocompromised patients; carries a worse prognosis than localised mucosal disease.

primarySymptoms: Unilateral nasal obstruction that progresses gradually over months to years, caused by a growing polyp that partially or completely blocks one nostril.
Recurrent epistaxis (nosebleeds), often provoked by nose-blowing, digital manipulation, or minor trauma to the friable polyp surface.
A visible pink-red fleshy mass protruding from the nostril, soft and mobile, that may be noticed by the patient or a parent in the case of an affected child.
Post-nasal drip and chronic mucus discharge from the affected nostril, sometimes blood-tinged, with an associated sensation of dripping at the back of the throat.
Hyponasal speech or a muffled nasal quality to the voice when the nasopharyngeal airway is narrowed by a polyp.
A foreign body sensation in the eye, conjunctival injection, and excessive tearing in patients with ocular rhinosporidiosis.
A pedunculated mass visible on the conjunctival surface that bleeds easily and may impair eyelid closure in ocular disease.
Dysphagia or a sensation of fullness in the throat when nasopharyngeal polyps grow toward the oropharynx.
Snoring and mouth-breathing, particularly in children, when nasopharyngeal obstruction is significant.
Cutaneous papules or wart-like nodules on the face, ear, or anogenital skin in disseminated cases — a presentation that should prompt consideration of immune assessment.
earlyWarningSigns: A single small, soft pink nodule visible just inside the nostril in a person from an endemic region who swims in rivers or ponds.
Intermittent unilateral nosebleeds in an otherwise healthy child or young adult from South Asia with no history of trauma.
A mildly irritated conjunctiva with a small fleshy vascular growth that does not resolve as a stye or chalazion would.
Very gradual unilateral nasal stuffiness that is not explained by allergy or common cold and does not respond to antihistamines.
emergencySigns: Complete bilateral nasal obstruction in a child with rhinosporidiosis requiring immediate airway assessment and urgent surgical planning.
Rapid polyp enlargement with massive epistaxis and haemodynamic signs of significant blood loss.
Evidence of intracranial or orbital extension in a patient with a rapidly expanding paranasal rhinosporidiosis lesion presenting with proptosis, visual change, or headache.
Signs of disseminated disease with fever, weight loss, and multi-site lesions in an immunocompromised patient.

causes.cause: Aquatic inoculation with Rhinosporidium seeberi endospores
causes.description: The primary route of infection: endospores released from mature sporangia in stagnant freshwater or muddy water penetrate intact or abraded nasal mucosa during bathing, diving, or wading, attaching to epithelial cells and maturing into sporangia within the submucosa.

causes.cause: Prolonged wading or bathing in stagnant freshwater
causes.description: Ponds, paddy fields, rivers with slow flow, and irrigation canals in South Asia serve as the environmental reservoir; repeated and prolonged immersion increases mucosal contact time with contaminated water and accumulates inoculation risk.

A minority of cases — notably in dry-climate areas of sub-Saharan Africa — may result from inhalation or nasal contact with R. seeberi-contaminated dust or soil particles rather than water, explaining rhinosporidiosis in populations without prominent freshwater exposure.

Manipulation or partial removal of a rhinosporidiosis polyp can release viable endospores that seed adjacent mucosal surfaces, the conjunctiva, or skin; inadequate surgical technique with spillage of polyp contents is the main cause of local recurrence and multi-site disease.

In rare cases, particularly in individuals with impaired cellular immunity, R. seeberi sporangia or endospores gain vascular access from mucosal sites and establish infection in skin, bones, liver, or genitourinary organs, producing disseminated rhinosporidiosis.

riskFactors.description: Regular bathing, wading, or diving in stagnant ponds, rivers, and paddy fields in India, Sri Lanka, and parts of Africa is the strongest risk factor; avoidance or use of protective nose clips during immersion reduces inoculation risk.

India (particularly Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Maharashtra) and Sri Lanka account for the vast majority of cases; sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South America contribute further clusters; the geographic concentration reflects where R. seeberi exists in the aquatic environment.

Men and boys make up approximately 80% of cases in most Indian series, consistent with differential rates of freshwater immersion through swimming and agricultural water work; the sex gap narrows in communities where women also participate in water-based activities.

Farmers involved in paddy cultivation, irrigation, and water management sustain frequent prolonged nasal immersion during planting and harvesting seasons, providing repeated inoculation opportunities.
Peak incidence in the first four decades of life correlates with maximum participation in recreational swimming and agricultural water work; disease onset in childhood is well documented.

HIV infection, prolonged corticosteroid therapy, and other causes of impaired cellular immunity substantially increase the risk of disseminated rhinosporidiosis, which is otherwise extremely rare in immunocompetent hosts.

riskFactors.factor: Previous rhinosporidiosis or inadequate surgical excision
riskFactors.description: Recurrence after incomplete excision or endospore spillage during surgery is well recognised; previous disease is a significant risk factor for multi-site involvement.

affectedDemographics: Boys and young men aged 10-40 from rural India (Tamil Nadu, Karnataka) and Sri Lanka
Agricultural workers involved in paddy cultivation and irrigation water management
Children who swim in freshwater ponds or rivers in endemic regions
Residents of sub-Saharan Africa (South Africa, Sudan, Cameroon) who report similar freshwater exposure
Immunocompromised individuals at risk for disseminated disease regardless of geographic origin
Travellers returning from endemic regions who present as rare imported cases in Europe or North America
diagnosisOverview: Rhinosporidiosis is diagnosed by histopathological examination of the excised polyp. The clinical presentation of a friable, vascular nasal polyp in a patient from South Asia with freshwater exposure is highly suggestive, but biopsy is essential for confirmation. Anterior rhinoscopy or nasal endoscopy reveals the characteristic strawberry-textured, sessile or pedunculated polyp with dilated vessels visible on the surface. The definitive histological finding is the presence of large sporangia (up to 350 µm in diameter), each containing thousands of small endospores (5-7 µm), embedded within a fibrovascular stroma with a mixed inflammatory infiltrate. Sporangia at various stages of maturation are typically seen together: immature sporangia have a thick chitinous wall and contain tightly packed endospores; mature sporangia show a thinned wall ready to rupture and release endospores. PAS and GMS stains highlight the sporangial walls strongly. No culture is possible, and no serological test is commercially available for R. seeberi. Polymerase chain reaction targeting R. seeberi 18S rRNA gene sequences has been published and is used at research centres for confirmation in atypical cases. The principal differential diagnoses are antrochoanal polyp (no sporangia on histology), inverted papilloma, juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma (primarily in young males, highly vascular, with characteristic CT findings), allergic polyps (pale oedematous stroma with eosinophils, no organisms), and hemangioma. Imaging (CT of the sinuses) is useful to delineate the extent of large polyps and plan surgical access but is not diagnostic.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualisation of the characteristic strawberry-textured, vascular polyp; identifies the site of attachment, number of lesions, and extent of nasopharyngeal involvement to guide surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A flexible or rigid nasal endoscope is passed through the nostril while you are seated; the procedure takes 5-10 minutes and may cause mild discomfort; local anaesthetic spray is sometimes used.

diagnosticTests.test: Excisional biopsy with histopathology (H&E, PAS, GMS)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive diagnosis: demonstrates the pathognomonic large sporangia (up to 350 µm) containing thousands of endospores within a fibrovascular stroma; no other pathogen produces this exact picture.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The polyp is surgically removed under local or general anaesthesia and sent to pathology; routine processing with H&E stain plus PAS and GMS takes 5-7 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Delineates the extent of large or complex polyps, rules out sinus wall erosion, identifies involvement of adjacent structures (orbit, skull base), and guides surgical approach planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 5-10 minute scan in a CT scanner; no contrast is usually needed; results reviewed before surgery.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Molecular confirmation of R. seeberi in atypical presentations or when histology is inconclusive; provides species-level identification at reference mycology or parasitology centres.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fresh or formalin-fixed tissue from the excised polyp is submitted to a specialised laboratory; turnaround is typically 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates conjunctival or lacrimal rhinosporidiosis; characterises the size and attachment point of the conjunctival mass and any associated corneal involvement.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A painless examination by an ophthalmologist using a microscope with a narrow beam of light; takes 10-15 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Chest X-ray and abdominal ultrasound in disseminated cases
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for visceral dissemination when multiple mucosal sites are involved or when the patient is immunocompromised; identifies pulmonary, hepatic, or lymph node lesions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard chest radiograph and abdominal ultrasound performed in the radiology department; painless and takes 15-30 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Surgical excision is the only effective treatment for rhinosporidiosis. Antifungal drugs do not work because Rhinosporidium seeberi is not a true fungus and lacks the ergosterol cell wall that azoles and polyenes target. The standard surgical approach for nasal rhinosporidiosis is endoscopic or direct excision of the polyp with its base, combined with electrocautery of the mucosal attachment site to destroy residual sporangia and reduce recurrence. Complete excision of the polyp stalk, with diathermy coagulation of the feeding vessels and mucosal base, is critical: incomplete removal or spillage of polyp contents releases viable endospores onto surrounding mucosa, producing multi-site recurrence. For ocular rhinosporidiosis, the conjunctival mass is excised under local anaesthesia with cryotherapy or diathermy to the base. For cutaneous and disseminated disease, wide excision is combined with systemic medical therapy. Dapsone (diaminodiphenyl sulfone) at 100 mg daily for a minimum of 6 months has been used as adjunctive therapy; it is thought to inhibit the maturation of sporangia and promote apoptosis of trophic forms rather than acting as an antifungal. Some retrospective case series from India report lower recurrence rates with dapsone supplementation after surgery, but randomised controlled trial evidence is lacking. Recurrence rates after adequate surgical excision range from 5-10% in specialist centres, and re-excision is required for recurrent disease. New aqueous exposures after treatment must be avoided to prevent re-infection.

medicalTreatments.name: Dapsone (adjunctive post-surgical therapy)
medicalTreatments.description: An antibacterial sulfonamide used adjunctively after surgical excision; taken at 100 mg daily for 6-12 months; proposed mechanism is inhibition of Rhinosporidium sporangium maturation and promotion of granuloma resolution rather than direct antimicrobial killing; not curative as monotherapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Retrospective case series from India suggest recurrence rates of approximately 5-10% with dapsone adjunct versus 15-25% with surgery alone; controlled trial data are lacking, so estimates have moderate certainty.

medicalTreatments.name: Electrocautery/diathermy of the surgical base
medicalTreatments.description: Electrocoagulation of the mucosal attachment point and surrounding 5 mm margin of mucosa after polyp excision; destroys residual sporangia and reduces local seeding; considered an integral part of surgical technique rather than a separate modality.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Associated with recurrence rates below 10% in published surgical series when combined with complete polyp excision; outcomes are substantially worse when base diathermy is omitted.

medicalTreatments.name: Cryotherapy (conjunctival rhinosporidiosis)
medicalTreatments.description: Liquid nitrogen or cryo-probe application to the excised conjunctival base; used in ocular disease as an adjunct to excision to destroy residual endospores and reduce recurrence without damage to the adjacent cornea.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Case series document recurrence rates below 10% with cryotherapy-assisted conjunctival excision; data come from small series at tertiary ophthalmology centres.

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic nasal polypectomy with base electrocautery
surgicalOptions.description: Rigid or flexible nasal endoscope-guided excision of the rhinosporidiosis polyp at its stalk, followed immediately by electrocautery of the mucosal base and a 5 mm margin; the standard procedure for nasal and nasopharyngeal disease; performed under general anaesthesia in children, local in cooperative adults.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence rates of 5-15% in specialist endoscopic surgical series; higher recurrence (up to 30%) with conventional non-endoscopic excision without base diathermy.

surgicalOptions.name: Open excision for large or complex lesions
surgicalOptions.description: Traditional surgical approach for large polyps, extensive nasopharyngeal involvement, or cases where endoscopy is limited by anatomical factors; requires general anaesthesia; provides wider access for complete excision but leaves larger mucosal defects.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Comparable cure rates to endoscopic excision when base diathermy is included; primarily used when endoscopic access is insufficient.

alternativeTreatments.name: Avoidance of freshwater exposure post-surgery
alternativeTreatments.description: The most important non-surgical measure for preventing recurrence; patients must avoid swimming in, bathing in, or wading through stagnant ponds and rivers in endemic regions after treatment, as re-exposure risks new inoculation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nose clips during water work
alternativeTreatments.description: Wearing protective nose clips during unavoidable freshwater exposure in endemic areas reduces nasal mucosal contact with contaminated water and is a practical prevention strategy for people who cannot avoid agricultural water work.

whySeeSpecialist: Rhinosporidiosis must be managed by an otolaryngologist (ENT specialist) because surgical excision requires precise technique to avoid endospore spillage. Incomplete or technically inadequate excision leads to multi-site recurrence that is harder to treat than the original lesion. Ophthalmologists should be involved for ocular disease. An infectious disease or tropical medicine specialist is valuable for disseminated or recurrent cases and for advising on dapsone therapy and monitoring.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an ENT surgeon with experience in nasal endoscopy and specifically with rhinosporidiosis, ideally at a specialist centre in endemic regions (India, Sri Lanka) or a tropical medicine unit with access to dermatopathology for histological confirmation. In non-endemic countries, seek a centre that has encountered rhinosporidiosis in travellers and can arrange pathology with PAS/GMS staining. Ask whether the surgeon uses base diathermy as a routine part of polypectomy technique, as this is the single most important recurrence-reducing step.
preventionStrategies: Avoid bathing, wading, or diving in stagnant ponds, paddy fields, or slow-moving rivers in endemic regions, particularly in South Asia and parts of Africa.
Wear nose clips and protective goggles during unavoidable freshwater immersion in endemic areas.
Cover any nasal abrasion or mucosal lesion before water contact in endemic regions; minor mucosal trauma facilitates inoculation.
After successful surgery, maintain permanent avoidance of endemic freshwater environments to prevent re-infection.
Community health education in endemic regions about the link between stagnant water bathing and rhinosporidiosis facilitates earlier presentation and reduces disease burden.
lifestyleModifications: Strict avoidance of freshwater swimming or wading in endemic regions post-treatment is the most effective lifestyle modification to prevent recurrence.
Take dapsone as prescribed — consistently, with food, and for the full prescribed duration — to minimise recurrence risk after surgery.
Report any new nasal mass, nosebleed, or conjunctival growth promptly; early re-excision of a small recurrence is far simpler than managing an established lesion.
Monitor for dapsone haematological side effects: methaemoglobinaemia causes cyanosis and shortness of breath; haemolytic anaemia causes pallor and fatigue — report either symptom immediately.
Maintain good hydration and avoid sun exposure if skin pigmentation changes occur with dapsone.
Children treated for rhinosporidiosis should be monitored at school health checks for nasal obstruction recurrence.

recommended: Folate-rich foods (leafy vegetables, legumes, fortified cereals) during dapsone therapy, which can cause folate-deficiency haemolysis in susceptible individuals
Iron-rich foods to counter the mild haemolytic tendency of dapsone
Adequate protein intake to support surgical wound healing
Plenty of water to support renal clearance of dapsone metabolites
avoid: Alcohol during dapsone therapy, which may worsen haematological side effects
Oxidant drugs taken concurrently with dapsone (e.g., primaquine) without medical supervision, as combined oxidant load increases methaemoglobinaemia risk

exerciseGuidelines: Physical activity is not restricted by rhinosporidiosis except during the first 2-4 weeks after nasal surgery when strenuous exertion and nose-blowing should be avoided to protect the healing excision site. Thereafter, normal exercise is encouraged. Swimming and water sports in freshwater bodies in endemic regions should be permanently avoided post-treatment. Swimming in chlorinated pools does not carry the same risk as stagnant natural water bodies.
dailyManagement: Gently irrigate the nasal passage with isotonic saline spray twice daily post-operatively to keep the surgical site moist and prevent crusting.
Take dapsone with food at the same time each day; a blood count and methaemoglobin level should be checked at baseline, 1 month, and 3 months into therapy.
Avoid digital manipulation of the nose or rubbing the eye after ocular excision to prevent autoinoculation.
Attend scheduled follow-up endoscopy at 3, 6, and 12 months post-surgery to detect early recurrence before it becomes large.
Inform the surgical team immediately if a new polyp is noticed, bleeding recurs, or nasal obstruction returns, as early re-excision markedly simplifies management.
prognosis: Rhinosporidiosis is benign in terms of systemic risk for immunocompetent patients: it does not spread to visceral organs and does not reduce life expectancy. After adequate surgical excision with base electrocautery, the recurrence rate at specialist centres is 5-15%, and most recurrences are manageable with re-excision. Without surgery, the polyp grows progressively and may eventually cause severe nasal obstruction, chronic epistaxis, and airway compromise requiring emergency intervention. Disseminated rhinosporidiosis in immunocompromised individuals carries a substantially worse prognosis, as multi-site disease is difficult to fully resect and dapsone provides incomplete suppression. The main determinants of outcome are surgical technique quality, post-surgical avoidance of endemic water exposure, and adherence to dapsone adjunctive therapy where prescribed.
recoveryTimeline: Recovery from uncomplicated nasal polypectomy takes 1-2 weeks; most patients are breathing normally within days. Post-operative nasal crusting and mild bleeding are common in the first 2 weeks. Follow-up endoscopy is recommended at 3, 6, and 12 months. Dapsone adjunctive therapy continues for 6-12 months post-surgery. Recurrence, when it occurs, most often appears within the first 12-24 months after surgery. Patients who remain free of disease for 3 years post-excision with avoidance of freshwater exposure have a very low long-term recurrence risk.
complications: Polyp recurrence — appears in 5-30% of cases depending on surgical technique; inadequate base excision or endospore spillage during surgery is the main cause; requires re-excision.
Multi-site seeding — autoinoculation during surgery or scratching spreads the infection to the nasopharynx, conjunctiva, or adjacent skin; presents as new polyps at distant mucosal sites.
Severe nasal obstruction — large untreated polyps can completely block one or both nostrils, particularly in children, causing mouth-breathing, sleep disturbance, and impaired speech.
Chronic epistaxis — recurrent nosebleeds from the friable polyp surface can cause iron-deficiency anaemia in children with large or longstanding lesions.

Disseminated rhinosporidiosis — rare multi-organ involvement in immunocompromised patients; may involve skin, bones, genitourinary tract, and liver; difficult to treat and associated with significant morbidity.

Dapsone toxicity — haemolytic anaemia and methaemoglobinaemia are the most clinically important side effects; G6PD-deficient patients are at substantially higher risk and should be screened before starting dapsone.

supportResources.name: WHO — Rhinosporidiosis information
supportResources.description: WHO guidance document on chronic subcutaneous infections and related waterborne pathogens including Rhinosporidium seeberi.

supportResources.name: CDC — Rhinosporidiosis
supportResources.description: CDC DPDx laboratory identification page for Rhinosporidium seeberi covering morphology, diagnostic features, and clinical background.

supportResources.name: DermNet NZ — Rhinosporidiosis
supportResources.description: Clinically reviewed dermatology and tropical medicine reference covering rhinosporidiosis presentation, pathology, and management.

sources.title: Arseculeratne SN. Recent advances in rhinosporidiosis and Rhinosporidium seeberi. Indian Journal of Medical Microbiology, 2002
Herr RA et al. Phylogenetic analysis of Rhinosporidium seeberi's 18S small-subunit ribosomal RNA gene. Journal of Eukaryotic Microbiology, 1999

confusedWithConditions.name: Nasal polyps (allergic)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Allergic nasal polyps are pale, oedematous, eosinophil-rich masses arising from sinus inflammation and respond to intranasal steroids; rhinosporidiosis polyps are vascular, friable, strawberry-textured and contain sporangia on biopsy — intranasal steroids have no effect on them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic rhinitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Allergic rhinitis causes nasal obstruction with pale swollen turbinates, watery discharge, sneezing, and itching; rhinosporidiosis presents with a single visible fleshy polyp, painless epistaxis, and no allergic symptoms; endoscopy and biopsy distinguish them definitively.

confusedWithConditions.name: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma is a malignant epithelial tumour presenting in the nasopharynx with cervical lymphadenopathy, cranial nerve involvement, and characteristic CT/MRI findings; rhinosporidiosis produces a benign vascular polyp without lymph node involvement; biopsy confirms the diagnosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Recurrent epistaxis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Recurrent epistaxis can arise from many causes including vascular malformations, coagulopathy, and HHT; rhinosporidiosis epistaxis is associated with a visible polyp mass on nasal endoscopy and has a specific histopathological diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: Nasal polyps
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common differential diagnosis; both present with nasal obstruction from intranasal masses, but nasal polyps are inflammatory and respond to steroids while rhinosporidiosis polyps require surgical excision.

relatedConditions.name: Eumycetoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Another chronic infection from environmental aquatic/soil organisms; classified under the same group of neglected tropical diseases; shares the need for surgical management as the primary treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Paracoccidioidomycosis
relatedConditions.relevance: A mucosal-affecting deep mycosis endemic in South America that can produce destructive nasopharyngeal lesions; distinguished from rhinosporidiosis by its systemic spread, characteristic histology, and response to itraconazole.

relatedConditions.name: Lobomycosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another chronic mucosal/cutaneous infection caused by an uncultivable aquatic protistan-related organism; shares the fundamental therapeutic principle that surgical excision is the primary curative option because antifungal drugs are ineffective.

faqs.question: What is rhinosporidiosis?
faqs.answer: Rhinosporidiosis is a chronic infection of the nasal mucosa and other wet surfaces caused by Rhinosporidium seeberi, an aquatic pathogen. It produces fleshy, vascular polyps that bleed easily and cause nasal blockage. It is found mainly in India and Sri Lanka.

faqs.question: How do you get rhinosporidiosis?
faqs.answer: The infection is acquired by bathing, wading, or diving in stagnant freshwater ponds or rivers where Rhinosporidium seeberi lives. It is not transmitted from person to person.

faqs.question: What does a rhinosporidiosis polyp look like?
faqs.answer: It appears as a soft, pink-red, strawberry-textured fleshy growth inside the nostril or on the conjunctiva. It bleeds easily when touched and grows slowly but progressively if untreated.

faqs.question: Is rhinosporidiosis a fungal infection?
faqs.answer: No. Despite its historical classification as a fungus, Rhinosporidium seeberi is now classified as an aquatic protist — it sits at the animal-fungal boundary. This is why antifungal drugs do not work against it.

faqs.question: How is rhinosporidiosis treated?
faqs.answer: Surgical excision of the polyp is the only effective treatment. The attachment point is then treated with electrocautery to destroy remaining organisms and reduce recurrence. Antifungal medicines do not work. Dapsone tablets are sometimes given afterward to reduce the chance of recurrence.

faqs.question: Can rhinosporidiosis come back after surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes, in 5-15% of cases at specialist centres. Recurrence is more common when the surgical base is not adequately treated with cautery or when the patient returns to bathing in endemic freshwater bodies after surgery.

faqs.question: Does rhinosporidiosis spread to other organs?
faqs.answer: In most people it stays confined to the nasal or conjunctival mucosa. Spread to other organs (disseminated rhinosporidiosis) is rare and occurs mainly in people with weakened immune systems.

faqs.question: Is rhinosporidiosis contagious?
faqs.answer: No. It cannot be caught from another person. The only source is the aquatic environment where the pathogen lives. Normal household contact with an affected person poses no risk.

faqs.question: What countries have rhinosporidiosis?
faqs.answer: India (especially Tamil Nadu and Karnataka), Sri Lanka, and Bangladesh account for the majority of cases. Cases also occur in parts of sub-Saharan Africa, South America, and the Middle East. Rare imported cases appear in Europe and North America.

faqs.question: Why do I get nosebleeds with rhinosporidiosis?
faqs.answer: The polyp surface is very vascular and fragile. Even light contact — nose-blowing, touching, or minor trauma — ruptures the surface blood vessels and causes bleeding. This stops once the polyp is surgically removed.

faqs.question: Can rhinosporidiosis affect the eyes?
faqs.answer: Yes. The conjunctiva and lacrimal sac are the second most common sites, causing a fleshy mass on the white of the eye, tearing, and foreign body sensation. Ocular rhinosporidiosis is treated by excision and cryotherapy to the base.

faqs.question: How is rhinosporidiosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: By biopsy of the polyp and examination under the microscope. The pathologist looks for large sacs (sporangia, up to 350 µm across) containing thousands of tiny spores — a pattern seen only with Rhinosporidium seeberi.

faqs.question: What is dapsone used for in rhinosporidiosis?
faqs.answer: Dapsone is an antibiotic tablet taken for 6-12 months after surgery to reduce the chance of recurrence. It is thought to prevent the spore sacs from maturing. It does not cure the infection on its own.

faqs.question: Can children get rhinosporidiosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children who swim in freshwater ponds or help with paddy farming in endemic areas are at risk. Nasal polyps in a child from South Asia with freshwater exposure should prompt consideration of rhinosporidiosis.

faqs.question: How do I prevent rhinosporidiosis?
faqs.answer: Avoid bathing or wading in stagnant ponds and rivers in endemic regions. Wear nose clips during unavoidable water contact. After surgery, permanently avoid endemic freshwater bodies to prevent re-infection.

faqs.question: Is rhinosporidiosis life-threatening?
faqs.answer: In healthy people it is not life-threatening, but untreated large polyps can cause complete nasal blockage requiring emergency intervention. The very rare disseminated form in immunocompromised patients can be serious.

faqs.question: What is the difference between rhinosporidiosis and a regular nasal polyp?
faqs.answer: Regular nasal polyps are pale, soft, and caused by chronic allergic inflammation; they respond to steroid sprays and do not bleed easily. Rhinosporidiosis polyps are vascular, bleed easily, come from freshwater exposure, and can only be treated surgically.

faqs.question: Does rhinosporidiosis cause pain?
faqs.answer: The polyp itself is usually painless. Nosebleeds and nasal obstruction are the main symptoms. Pain or facial pressure may develop if secondary sinus involvement occurs, but rhinosporidiosis itself does not cause significant pain in most patients.

faqs.question: Who should operate on a rhinosporidiosis polyp?
faqs.answer: An ENT (ear, nose, and throat) surgeon with nasal endoscopy experience and familiarity with rhinosporidiosis technique. Proper base cautery is essential and is a specialist skill; inadequate technique leads to recurrence.

faqs.question: Can rhinosporidiosis cause skin lesions?
faqs.answer: In rare cases, especially in immunocompromised patients, Rhinosporidium can spread to the skin via autoinoculation or bloodstream spread, causing wart-like papules. Skin involvement always warrants immune assessment and specialist review.

metaDescription: Rhinosporidiosis causes vascular nasal polyps from freshwater exposure in South Asia. Learn symptoms, biopsy diagnosis, and surgical treatment with dapsone.
